bins.name: PUHCA Exemption for IPP Direct Sales
bins.desc: ENGIE supports a blanket PUHCA exemption that would let IPPs sell electricity directly to hybrid large loads without losing exemptions for upstream owners. The current requirement to route sales through a third-party retail provider adds cost and inefficiency. A blanket exemption, rather than transaction-by-transaction filings, would remove a concrete barrier to hybrid large load interconnection.
quotes: ENGIE has reviewed and supports initial comments recommending that the Commission provide blanket PUHCA exemptions for IPPs that sell directly to hybrid facility loads.

Having to "sleeve" the transactions creates unnecessary barriers and is a significant impediment to power sale arrangements between IPPs and large loads because it increases costs and reduces transaction efficiency.

Reforms to PUHCA that allow IPPs to make direct sales to large loads without losing PUHCA exemptions for upstream owners would facilitate the interconnection and service of new large loads to the electric grid.
Permitting IPPs to file only one request, instead of individual requests for each transaction would greatly reduce the barriers to the development of hybrid large load interconnections.

bins.name: Federal Authority and Jurisdiction Limits
bins.desc: ENGIE wants clear FERC jurisdiction to enable large load interconnection rules, but insists the final rule stay within federal authority and not encroach on state jurisdiction. Clear, legally defensible rules are treated as a prerequisite for long-term investment.
quotes: Reforms to PUHCA that allow IPPs to make direct sales to large loads without losing PUHCA exemptions for upstream owners would facilitate the interconnection and service of new large loads to the electric grid.
Permitting IPPs to file only one request, instead of individual requests for each transaction would greatly reduce the barriers to the development of hybrid large load interconnections.
Clear, consistent and legally defensible rules are essential for IPPs and all stakeholders to foster long-term investment and to bring large load interconnection to fruition.
One way to ensure validity and durability of the final rule is to respect and account for the limits of federal authority and avoid interfering with areas under state jurisdiction and control.

bins.name: Durable Rule via NOPR Process
bins.desc: ENGIE urges the Commission to issue a NOPR with a reasonable comment period rather than moving directly to a final rule. Providing draft regulatory language gives stakeholders the ability to comment meaningfully, and issuing the rule through standard notice-and-comment reduces litigation risk.
quotes: Clear, consistent and legally defensible rules are essential for IPPs and all stakeholders to foster long-term investment and to bring large load interconnection to fruition.
One way to ensure validity and durability of the final rule is to respect and account for the limits of federal authority and avoid interfering with areas under state jurisdiction and control.
A second way to ensure validity and durability of the final rule is to issue a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR") with a reasonable timeframe for comment.
it will be important for stakeholders to have a better sense of a potential framework and draft regulatory language on which to provide further comment before finalization.

bins.name: Transaction Sleeving Inefficiency
bins.desc: ENGIE describes how EWG status forces IPPs to use a third-party retail provider to sell power to large loads, a practice called sleeving. This adds cost and reduces efficiency and is framed as a structural impediment to direct IPP-to-load arrangements.
quotes: Having to "sleeve" the transactions creates unnecessary barriers and is a significant impediment to power sale arrangements between IPPs and large loads because it increases costs and reduces transaction efficiency.
